title: Quiz: optimistic vs pessimistic locking
prompt_md: You're updating rows that are **rarely** touched by two requests at once (low contention). Which locking strategy fits best, and why?
options: Optimistic locking — no lock is held; you just check a version number at write time, which is cheap and assumes conflicts are rare
Pessimistic locking — always lock the row up front so no one can ever conflict, regardless of how rare conflicts are
Neither — low-contention data can never have a race condition
Optimistic locking — because it physically prevents other transactions from reading the row
explanation_md: **Optimistic locking** is the right fit for low contention. You don't hold a lock; you read a `version`, do your work, and at write time check the version is still what you read (`... WHERE version = 5`). If nothing changed — the common case when conflicts are rare — the write succeeds cheaply; if someone got there first, the update hits 0 rows and you retry. Pessimistic locking (`SELECT ... FOR UPDATE`) makes others wait and is overkill when conflicts are unlikely; it's better reserved for high-contention hotspots. Note optimistic locking does NOT block others from reading.
